mn119:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn119:10.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, just as a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse thrown away in a charnel ground being eaten by crows, being eaten by hawks, being eaten by vultures, being eaten by kites, being eaten by dogs, being eaten by tigers, being eaten by leopards, being eaten by jackals, or being eaten by various kinds of creatures.
mn119:10.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn119:10.3: 'This body too has this as its nature, will become like this, and is not beyond this.'
mn119:10.5: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:11-14.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, just as a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse thrown away in a charnel ground, a skeleton with flesh and blood, held together by sinews …
mn119:11-14.2: a skeleton without flesh, smeared with blood, held together by sinews …
mn119:11-14.3: a skeleton without flesh and blood, held together by sinews …
mn119:11-14.4: bones disconnected, scattered in all directions—here a hand-bone, there a foot-bone, here an ankle-bone, there a shin-bone, here a thigh-bone, there a hip-bone, here a rib-bone, there a backbone, here a shoulder-bone, there a neck-bone, here a jaw-bone, there a tooth-bone, here a skull.
mn119:11-14.5: He compares this very body with it:
mn119:11-14.6: 'This body too has this as its nature, will become like this, and is not beyond this.'
mn119:11-14.8: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:15-17.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, just as a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse thrown away in a charnel ground—
mn119:15-17.2: bones white, the color of shells …
mn119:15-17.3: bones heaped up, more than a year old …
mn119:15-17.4: bones rotten and reduced to powder.
mn119:15-17.5: He compares this very body with it:
mn119:15-17.6: 'This body too has this as its nature, will become like this, and is not beyond this.'
mn119:15-17.9: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:18.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … he enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*.
mn119:18.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajena pītisukhena*], so that there is no part of his whole body untouched by *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajena pītisukhena*].
mn119:18.3: Just as, bhikkhus, a skilled bath attendant, or a bath attendant’s apprentice, might heap bath powder in a bronze bowl and knead it, sprinkling it again and again with water, so that the ball of bath powder would be moistened through, permeated through, inside and out infused with moisture, but would not drip;
mn119:18.4: just so, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajena pītisukhena*]; so that there is no part of his whole body untouched by *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajena pītisukhena*].
mn119:18.7: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:19.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, with the settling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*] … he enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*.
mn119:19.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijena pītisukhena*]; so that there is no part of his whole body untouched by *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijena pītisukhena*].
mn119:19.3: Just as, bhikkhus, there might be a deep lake fed by springs, with no inflow of water from the east, no inflow of water from the west, no inflow of water from the north, and no inflow of water from the south, and the sky would not from time to time send down a proper shower of rain; but a cool stream of water, welling up from that very lake, would drench, steep, fill, and pervade that very lake with cool water, so that there would be no part of the whole lake untouched by cool water;
mn119:19.4: just so, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijena pītisukhena*], so that there is no part of his whole body untouched by *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijena pītisukhena*].
mn119:19.6: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:2.1: Then, when several *bhikkhus* had returned from the almsround after the meal and were seated together in the assembly hall, this discussion arose among them:
mn119:2.10: This, bhante, was the discussion left unfinished for us, and then the *Bhagavā* arrived."
mn119:2.3: How great it is that by the *Bhagavā*—knowing and seeing, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one—*sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*], when developed and made much of, has been said to be of great fruit and great benefit."
mn119:2.4: And this discussion of those *bhikkhus* was left unfinished when, in the evening, the *Bhagavā* emerged from seclusion and went to the assembly hall. Having gone there, he sat down on the seat laid out.
mn119:2.6: "Bhikkhus, for what discussion are you now seated together, and what discussion was left unfinished?"
mn119:2.7: "Here, bhante, when we had returned from the almsround after the meal and were seated together in the assembly hall, this discussion arose among us:
mn119:2.9: How great it is that by the *Bhagavā*—knowing and seeing, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one—*sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*], when developed and made much of, has been said to be of great fruit and great benefit.'
mn119:20.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, with the fading of *pīti* … he enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*.
mn119:20.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *sukha* without *pīti* [*nippītikena sukhena*], so that there is no part of his whole body untouched by *sukha* without *pīti* [*nippītikena sukhena*].
mn119:20.3: Just as, bhikkhus, in a pond of blue lotuses, or red lotuses, or white lotuses, some blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses, born in the water and grown in the water, might thrive while submerged in the water, and from their tips to their roots be drenched, steeped, filled, and pervaded with cool water, so that there would be no part of all those blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses untouched by cool water;
mn119:20.4: just so, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *sukha* without *pīti* [*nippītikena sukhena*], so that there is no part of his whole body untouched by *sukha* without *pīti* [*nippītikena sukhena*].
mn119:20.6: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:21.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, with the giving up of *sukha* … he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn119:21.2: He sits pervading this very body with a purified, bright mind, so that there is no part of his whole body untouched by the purified, bright mind.
mn119:21.3: Just as, bhikkhus, a person might sit wrapped from head to foot in a white cloth, so that there would be no part of his whole body untouched by the white cloth;
mn119:21.4: just so, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* sits pervading this very body with a purified, bright mind, so that there is no part of his whole body untouched by the purified, bright mind.
mn119:21.5: For him, dwelling thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, those thoughts of relish connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:21.6: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself stands firm inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn119:21.7: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:22.1: For anyone, bhikkhus, whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, all skillful *dhammas* conducive to knowledge are included within it.
mn119:22.2: Just as, bhikkhus, for anyone who has mentally encompassed the great ocean, all streams, whatever rivers flow to the sea, are included within it;
mn119:22.3: just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, all skillful *dhammas* conducive to knowledge are included within it.
mn119:23.1: For anyone, bhikkhus, whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has not been developed and not made much of, Māra finds an opening there; Māra finds a foothold there.
mn119:23.2: Just as, bhikkhus, a person might throw a heavy stone ball onto a lump of wet clay.
mn119:23.4: would that heavy stone ball find an opening in the lump of wet clay?"
mn119:23.6: "Just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has not been developed and not made much of, Māra finds an opening there; Māra finds a foothold there.
mn119:24.1: Just as, bhikkhus, there is dry wood, dry as kindling;
mn119:24.2: then a person might come carrying an upper fire-stick:
mn119:24.3: 'I will produce fire; I will make heat appear.'
mn119:24.5: could that person, by rubbing with the upper fire-stick against that dry wood, dry as kindling, produce fire and make heat appear?"
mn119:24.7: "Just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has not been developed and not made much of, Māra finds an opening there; Māra finds a foothold there.
mn119:25.1: Just as, bhikkhus, an empty water jar, hollow and void, might be placed on a stand;
mn119:25.2: then a person might come carrying a load of water.
mn119:25.4: could that person find a place to pour in the water?"
mn119:25.6: "Just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has not been developed and not made much of, Māra finds an opening there; Māra finds a foothold there.
mn119:26.1: For anyone, bhikkhus, whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, Māra finds no opening there; Māra finds no foothold there.
mn119:26.2: Just as, bhikkhus, a person might throw a light ball of string against a door-panel made entirely of heartwood.
mn119:26.4: would that person make that light ball of string find an opening in that door-panel made entirely of heartwood?"
mn119:26.6: "Just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, Māra finds no opening there; Māra finds no foothold there.
mn119:27.1: Just as, bhikkhus, there is wet wood, full of sap;
mn119:27.2: then a person might come carrying an upper fire-stick:
mn119:27.3: 'I will produce fire; I will make heat appear.'
mn119:27.5: could that person, by rubbing with the upper fire-stick against that wet, sappy wood, produce fire and make heat appear?"
mn119:27.7: "Just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, Māra finds no opening there; Māra finds no foothold there.
mn119:28.1: Just as, bhikkhus, a water jar full, filled to the brim, enough for a crow to drink, might be placed on a stand;
mn119:28.2: then a person might come carrying a load of water.
mn119:28.4: could that person find a place to pour in the water?"
mn119:28.6: "Just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, Māra finds no opening there; Māra finds no foothold there.
mn119:29.1: For anyone, bhikkhus, whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, whatever *dhamma* is to be realized by direct knowledge, if he inclines his mind [*citta*] toward its direct realization, then and there he attains the capacity to witness it, when the appropriate dimension [*āyatana*] is present.
mn119:29.2: Just as, bhikkhus, a water jar full, filled to the brim, enough for a crow to drink, might be placed on a stand.
mn119:29.3: "If a strong person were to tip it in whatever direction, would water come out?"
mn119:29.5: "Just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, whatever *dhamma* is to be realized by direct knowledge, if he inclines his mind [*citta*] toward its direct realization, then and there he attains the capacity to witness it, when the appropriate dimension [*āyatana*] is present.
mn119:3.1: "And how, bhikkhus, when *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] is developed and made much of, does it become of great fruit and great benefit?
mn119:30.1: Just as, bhikkhus, on level ground there might be a square pond with embankments, full of water, filled to the brim, enough for a crow to drink.
mn119:30.2: "If a strong person were to open the embankment on whatever side, would water come out?"
mn119:30.4: "Just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, whatever *dhamma* is to be realized by direct knowledge, if he inclines his mind [*citta*] toward its direct realization, then and there he attains the capacity to witness it, when the appropriate dimension [*āyatana*] is present.
mn119:31.1: Just as, bhikkhus, at a four-way crossroads on level ground, a thoroughbred chariot, yoked and ready, might be standing with goad lowered.
mn119:31.2: An expert driver, a horse-trainer, skilled as a charioteer, might mount it and, taking the reins in his left hand and the goad in his right hand, might drive it out and drive it back wherever and however he wishes.
mn119:31.3: Just so, bhikkhus, for anyone whose *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed and made much of, whatever *dhamma* is to be realized by direct knowledge, if he inclines his mind [*citta*] toward its direct realization, then and there he attains the capacity to witness it, when the appropriate dimension [*āyatana*] is present.
mn119:32.1: When *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāya satiyā*] has been practiced, developed, made much of, made a vehicle, made a basis, established, familiarized, and well undertaken, bhikkhus, ten benefits can be expected.
mn119:33.1: One becomes able to endure discontent and delight, and one does not give in to discontent; when discontent arises, one dwells having overcome it.
mn119:34.1: One becomes able to endure fear and dread, and one does not give in to fear and dread; when fear and dread arise, one dwells having overcome them.
mn119:35.1: One becomes tolerant of cold and heat, hunger and thirst, the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and creeping things, of rude and unwelcome ways of speech; and one becomes capable of enduring arisen bodily *vedanās* that are painful, sharp, severe, bitter, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening.
mn119:36.1: One becomes one who obtains at will, without trouble and without difficulty, the four *jhānas*, which are higher-mind pleasant dwellings in this very life [*diṭṭhadhammasukhavihārānaṁ*].
mn119:37.1: He personally experiences the many kinds of supernormal power. Being one, he becomes many; being many, he becomes one; he appears … he wields mastery with the body even as far as the Brahmā world.
mn119:38.1: With the divine ear-element, purified and surpassing the human, he hears both kinds of sounds, divine and human, whether far or near …
mn119:39.1: Having encompassed with his own mind the mind [*citta*] of other beings, of other persons, he understands it.
mn119:39.12: or a mind [*citta*] with something higher beyond it …
mn119:39.15: or a mind [*citta*] not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ*] …
mn119:39.17: or a mind [*citta*] not liberated as 'a mind [*citta*] not liberated.'
mn119:39.2: He understands a mind [*citta*] with lust as 'a mind [*citta*] with lust';
mn119:4.1: Here, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, sits down, folding his legs crosswise, setting his body erect, and establishing *sati* before him [*parimukhaṁ satiṁ upaṭṭhapetvā*].
mn119:4.2: He breathes in with *sati*; he breathes out with *sati*.
mn119:4.3: Breathing in long, he understands: 'I breathe in long'; or breathing out long, he understands: 'I breathe out long.'
mn119:4.4: Breathing in short, he understands: 'I breathe in short'; or breathing out short, he understands: 'I breathe out short.'
mn119:4.5: He trains thus: 'Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in'; he trains thus: 'Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out.'
mn119:4.6: He trains thus: 'Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe in'; he trains thus: 'Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe out.'
mn119:4.7: For him, dwelling thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, those thoughts of relish connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:4.8: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself stands firm inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn119:4.9: This is how, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:40.1: He recollects his many kinds of past lives, that is: one birth, two births … Thus with aspects and details he recollects his many kinds of past lives.
mn119:41.1: With the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, he sees beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in good destinations and bad destinations, and he understands beings as going according to their *kamma*.
mn119:42.1: With the destruction of the *āsavas*, he dwells having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] without *āsavas* [*anāsavaṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*].
mn119:43.1: When *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāya satiyā*] has been practiced, developed, made much of, made a vehicle, made a basis, established, familiarized, and well undertaken, bhikkhus, these ten benefits can be expected."
mn119:43.3: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn119:43.4: The ninth is finished: *Sati* Directed to the Body.
mn119:5.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, when walking, a *bhikkhu* understands: 'I am walking'; when standing, he understands: 'I am standing'; when sitting, he understands: 'I am sitting'; when lying down, he understands: 'I am lying down.'
mn119:5.2: Or however his body is disposed, he understands it accordingly.
mn119:5.3: For him, dwelling thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, those thoughts of relish connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:5.4: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself stands firm inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn119:5.5: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:6.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when going forward and returning, acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when looking ahead and looking away, acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when bending and stretching, acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in wearing the outer robe and carrying the bowl and robe, acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting, acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in defecating and urinating, acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when walking, standing, sitting, falling asleep, waking up, speaking, and keeping silent.
mn119:6.2: For him, dwelling thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, those thoughts of relish connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:6.3: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself stands firm inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn119:6.4: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:7.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body from the soles of the feet upward and from the crown of the head downward, bounded by skin, as full of many kinds of impurity:
mn119:7.10: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself stands firm inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn119:7.11: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:7.2: 'In this body there are head-hairs, body-hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, membranes, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, mucus, synovial fluid, and urine.'
mn119:7.3: Just as, bhikkhus, there might be a bag open at both ends, full of various kinds of grain, that is to say—
mn119:7.4: rice, paddy, mung beans, beans, sesame, and husked rice—
mn119:7.5: and a sharp-eyed person might untie it and review it:
mn119:7.6: 'These are rice, these are paddy, these are mung beans, these are beans, these are sesame, these are husked rice';
mn119:7.7: just so, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body from the soles of the feet upward and from the crown of the head downward, bounded by skin, as full of many kinds of impurity:
mn119:7.8: 'In this body there are head-hairs, body-hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, membranes, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, mucus, synovial fluid, and urine.'
mn119:7.9: For him, dwelling thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, those thoughts of relish connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:8.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, however it stands and however it is disposed, in terms of elements:
mn119:8.2: 'In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.'
mn119:8.3: Just as, bhikkhus, a skilled butcher, or a butcher’s apprentice, having slaughtered a cow, might sit at a crossroads having divided it into portions;
mn119:8.4: just so, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, however it stands and however it is disposed, in terms of elements:
mn119:8.5: 'In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.'
mn119:8.6: For him, dwelling thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, those thoughts of relish connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:8.7: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself stands firm inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn119:8.8: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:9.1: Furthermore, bhikkhus, just as a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse thrown away in a charnel ground, dead for one day, or two days, or three days, bloated, livid, and festering.
mn119:9.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn119:9.3: 'This body too has this as its nature, will become like this, and is not beyond this.'
mn119:9.4: For him, dwelling thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, those thoughts of relish connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:9.5: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself stands firm inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn119:9.6: In this way too, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* develops *sati* directed to the body [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
